140→// macos uses DYLD_PRINT_LIBRARIES, not LD_DEBUG, so disable on macos at the moment.
   141→// On modern Android (Bionic, API 37+), LD_DEBUG is supported and behaves similarly to glibc.
   142→// On older Android versions (Bionic, API < 37), LD_DEBUG uses integer values instead of strings
   143→// and is sometimes disabled. Disable test on Android for now.
   144→// musl libc dynamic loader does not support LD_DEBUG, so disable on musl targets as well.
   145→#[cfg(all(
   146→    not(target_os = "windows"),
   147→    not(target_os = "openbsd"),
   148→    not(target_os = "macos"),
   149→    not(target_os = "android"),
   150→    not(target_env = "musl")
   151→))]
   152→#[test]
   153→fn test_libstdbuf_preload() {
   154→    use std::process::Command;
   155→
   156→    // Run a simple program with LD_DEBUG=symbols to verify that libstdbuf is loaded correctly
   157→    // and that there are no architecture mismatches when preloading the library.
   158→    // Note: This does not check which setvbuf implementation is used, as our libstdbuf does not override setvbuf.
   159→    // for https://github.com/uutils/coreutils/issues/6591
   160→
   161→    let scene = TestScenario::new(util_name!());
   162→    let coreutils_bin = &scene.bin_path;
   163→
   164→    // Test with our own echo (should have the correct architecture even when cross-compiled using cross-rs,
   165→    // in which case the "system" echo will be the host architecture)
   166→    let uutils_echo_cmd = format!(
   167→        "LD_DEBUG=symbols {} stdbuf -oL {} echo test 2>&1",
   168→        coreutils_bin.display(),
   169→        coreutils_bin.display()
   170→    );
   171→    let uutils_output = Command::new("sh")
   172→        .arg("-c")
   173→        .arg(&uutils_echo_cmd)
   174→        .output()
   175→        .expect("Failed to run uutils echo test");
   176→
   177→    let uutils_debug = String::from_utf8_lossy(&uutils_output.stdout);
   178→
   179→    // Check if libstdbuf.so / libstdbuf.dylib is in the lookup path.

1→// This file is part of the uutils coreutils package.
     2→//
     3→// For the full copyright and license information, please view the LICENSE
     4→// file that was distributed with this source code.
     5→//! All utils return exit with an exit code. Usually, the following scheme is used:
     6→//! * `0`: succeeded
     7→//! * `1`: minor problems
     8→//! * `2`: major problems
     9→//!
    10→//! This module provides types to reconcile these exit codes with idiomatic Rust error
    11→//! handling. This has a couple advantages over manually using [`std::process::exit`]:
    12→//! 1. It enables the use of `?`, `map_err`, `unwrap_or`, etc. in `uumain`.
    13→//! 1. It encourages the use of [`UResult`]/[`Result`] in functions in the utils.
    14→//! 1. The error messages are largely standardized across utils.
    15→//! 1. Standardized error messages can be created from external result types
    16→//!    (i.e. [`std::io::Result`] & `clap::ClapResult`).
    17→//! 1. [`set_exit_code`] takes away the burden of manually tracking exit codes for non-fatal errors.
    18→//!
    19→//! # Usage
    20→//! The signature of a typical util should be:
    21→//! ```ignore
    22→//! fn uumain(args: impl uucore::Args) -> UResult<()> {
    23→//!     ...
    24→//! }
    25→//! ```
    26→//! [`UResult`] is a simple wrapper around [`Result`] with a custom error trait: [`UError`]. The
    27→//! most important difference with types implementing [`std::error::Error`] is that [`UError`]s
    28→//! can specify the exit code of the program when they are returned from `uumain`:
    29→//! * When `Ok` is returned, the code set with [`set_exit_code`] is used as exit code. If
    30→//!   [`set_exit_code`] was not used, then `0` is used.
    31→//! * When `Err` is returned, the code corresponding with the error is used as exit code and the
    32→//!   error message is displayed.
    33→//!
    34→//! Additionally, the errors can be displayed manually with the [`crate::show`] and [`crate::show_if_err`] macros:
    35→//! ```ignore
    36→//! let res = Err(USimpleError::new(1, "Error!!"));
    37→//! show_if_err!(res);
    38→//! // or
    39→//! if let Err(e) = res {
    40→//!    show!(e);
    41→//! }
    42→//! ```
    43→//!
    44→//! **Note**: The [`crate::show`] and [`crate::show_if_err`] macros set the exit code of the program using
    45→//! [`set_exit_code`]. See the documentation on that function for more information.
    46→//!
    47→//! # Guidelines
    48→//! * Use error types from `uucore` where possible.
    49→//! * Add error types to `uucore` if an error appears in multiple utils.
    50→//! * Prefer proper custom error types over [`ExitCode`] and [`USimpleError`].
    51→//! * [`USimpleError`] may be used in small utils with simple error handling.
    52→//! * Using [`ExitCode`] is not recommended but can be useful for converting utils to use
    53→//!   [`UResult`].
    54→
    55→// spell-checker:ignore uioerror rustdoc
    56→
    57→use std::{
    58→    error::Error,
    59→    fmt::{Display, Formatter},
    60→    sync::atomic::{AtomicI32, Ordering},
    61→};
    62→
    63→static EXIT_CODE: AtomicI32 = AtomicI32::new(0);
    64→
    65→/// Get the last exit code set with [`set_exit_code`].
    66→/// The default value is `0`.
    67→pub fn get_exit_code() -> i32 {
    68→    EXIT_CODE.load(Ordering::SeqCst)
    69→}
    70→
    71→/// Set the exit code for the program if `uumain` returns `Ok(())`.
    72→///
    73→/// This function is most useful for non-fatal errors, for example when applying an operation to
    74→/// multiple files:
    75→/// ```ignore
    76→/// use uucore::error::{UResult, set_exit_code};
    77→///
    78→/// fn uumain(args: impl uucore::Args) -> UResult<()> {
    79→///     ...
    80→///     for file in files {
    81→///         let res = some_operation_that_might_fail(file);
    82→///         match res {
    83→///             Ok() => {},
    84→///             Err(_) => set_exit_code(1),
    85→///         }
    86→///     }
    87→///     Ok(()) // If any of the operations failed, 1 is returned.
    88→/// }
    89→/// ```
    90→pub fn set_exit_code(code: i32) {
    91→    EXIT_CODE.store(code, Ordering::SeqCst);
    92→}
    93→
    94→/// Result type that should be returned by all utils.
    95→pub type UResult<T> = Result<T, Box<dyn UError>>;
    96→
    97→/// Custom errors defined by the utils and `uucore`.
    98→///
    99→/// All errors should implement [`std::error::Error`], [`std::fmt::Display`] and
   100→/// [`std::fmt::Debug`] and have an additional `code` method that specifies the
   101→/// exit code of the program if the error is returned from `uumain`.
   102→///
   103→/// An example of a custom error from `ls`:
   104→///
   105→/// ```
   106→/// use uucore::{
   107→///     display::Quotable,
   108→///     error::{UError, UResult}
   109→/// };
   110→/// use std::{
   111→///     error::Error,
   112→///     fmt::{Display, Debug},
   113→///     path::PathBuf
   114→/// };
   115→///
   116→/// #[derive(Debug)]
   117→/// enum LsError {
   118→///     InvalidLineWidth(String),
   119→///     NoMetadata(PathBuf),
   120→/// }

content.text: Fixed `cat` emitting `EPIPE: Broken pipe` when the downstream side of a pipeline closes early. Changes made: - `src/uu/cat/src/splice.rs`: when the splice fast-path falls back to `copy_exact()`, treat `EPIPE` from `write()` as a normal condition and stop copying without error. - `src/uu/cat/src/cat.rs`: also treat `std::io::ErrorKind::BrokenPipe` as non-fatal in the non-splice write paths (write loop + flush), and avoid recording it as a per-file error. Verification: - Added a temporary regression test reproducing `cat /dev/zero | head -c1` behavior, confirmed it failed before the splice fix and passed after. - Removed the temporary test and its module hook afterward, per instructions. - Ran an existing `cat` test to ensure no regressions in the harness.

trajectory.result: Fixed `cat` emitting `EPIPE: Broken pipe` when the downstream side of a pipeline closes early. Changes made: - `src/uu/cat/src/splice.rs`: when the splice fast-path falls back to `copy_exact()`, treat `EPIPE` from `write()` as a normal condition and stop copying without error. - `src/uu/cat/src/cat.rs`: also treat `std::io::ErrorKind::BrokenPipe` as non-fatal in the non-splice write paths (write loop + flush), and avoid recording it as a per-file error. Verification: - Added a temporary regression test reproducing `cat /dev/zero | head -c1` behavior, confirmed it failed before the splice fix and passed after. - Removed the temporary test and its module hook afterward, per instructions. - Ran an existing `cat` test to ensure no regressions in the harness.
